skill.name: conversation-memory
skill.description: Guides the design of persistent memory systems for LLM conversations, including short-term, long-term, and entity-based memory.
skill.summary: Design LLM conversation memory with clear storage, retrieval, consolidation, and user isolation patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks on SKILL.md line 61 as shell execution and front matter on line 3 as weak cryptography. Review found no executable code, no cryptographic implementation, no network access, and no prompt injection attempt. The skill is a guidance-only Markdown file with low residual risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Interpreted as Commands
low_findings.description: The related-skills list on line 61 uses Markdown inline code formatting for skill names. It does not define or execute shell, Ruby, or other external commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is plain Markdown content in a documentation file. No executable script, command invocation, or user input flow exists at this location.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The front matter description on line 3 discusses persistent memory for LLM conversations. It does not contain a cryptographic algorithm or security-sensitive hashing logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is descriptive metadata only. No cryptographic API, hashing function, encoded payload, or implementation logic is present.

content.value_statement: Long-running AI conversations lose useful context when memory is unmanaged. This skill helps teams design memory tiers, retrieval rules, and isolation controls for LLM assistants.
actual_capabilities: Explains short-term, long-term, and entity-based memory patterns for LLM conversations.
Helps define retrieval strategies that surface relevant memories in prompts.
Identifies anti-patterns such as remembering everything or using one memory store.
Highlights lifecycle controls for unbounded memory growth.
Calls out strict user isolation as a critical memory design requirement.
limitations: Provides design guidance only and does not include runnable memory storage code.
Does not choose a database, vector store, or embedding model for a project.
Does not include privacy policy text or compliance controls.
Requires separate implementation and testing before production use.

use_cases.title: Plan Assistant Memory Architecture
use_cases.description: Define memory tiers, retention rules, and retrieval behavior before building a persistent AI assistant.

use_cases.title: Improve Chat Continuity
use_cases.description: Use entity memory and relevant retrieval to make repeated conversations feel consistent without overloading context.

use_cases.title: Review Memory Safety Risks
use_cases.description: Check whether a memory design handles growth, relevance, forgetting, and strict user separation.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Memory Needs
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new assistant
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this assistant concept and list what should use short-term memory, long-term memory, and entity memory. Explain why each item belongs there.

prompt_templates.title: Design Retrieval Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Create retrieval rules for this conversation memory system. Include relevance signals, freshness rules, and when memories should not be injected into the prompt.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Memory Anti-Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this memory architecture for common failures. Focus on unbounded growth, irrelevant retrieval, missing consolidation, and cross-user memory exposure.

prompt_templates.title: Specify Production Controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this memory design into production requirements. Include storage boundaries, retention lifecycle, access controls, evaluation metrics, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: A tutoring assistant should remember student goals, recent mistakes, and preferred explanation style.
output_examples.output: The response separates recent mistakes into short-term memory, durable learning goals into long-term memory, and preferences into entity memory. It also suggests relevance checks before injecting memories.

output_examples.input: A support assistant retrieves every past ticket summary for each new chat.
output_examples.output: The response flags the design as excessive retrieval. It recommends filtering by account, product, recency, issue type, and confirmed relevance.

output_examples.input: A team wants one shared memory database for all users.
output_examples.output: The response identifies cross-user exposure as a critical risk. It recommends tenant isolation, access checks, and tests that prove memories cannot leak between users.

best_practices: Define separate memory tiers with clear purpose, retention, and retrieval rules.
Retrieve only memories that are relevant to the current user, task, and conversation.
Test user isolation and deletion behavior before enabling persistent memory.
anti_patterns: Do not store every conversation detail without lifecycle management.
Do not inject memories into prompts without relevance scoring.
Do not mix memories from different users or tenants in one accessible context.

faq.question: Does this skill implement a memory database?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance for memory systems, not runnable storage code.

faq.question: Can this help with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide memory design discussions and implementation planning across those tools.

faq.question: What memory types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers short-term memory, long-term memory, entity memory, retrieval, persistence, and consolidation.

faq.question: Does it address privacy risks?
faq.answer: It highlights user isolation and memory lifecycle risks, but privacy controls must be implemented separately.

faq.question: When should memories be retrieved?
faq.answer: Memories should be retrieved when they are relevant to the current user, task, and conversation context.

faq.question: What is the main production risk?
faq.answer: The main risk is exposing one user’s memories to another user through weak isolation or retrieval logic.
